text: The surgeon inserts a calibrating bougie into the stomach, then identifies the stomach and begins dissecting the short gastric vessels connecting the stomach to the spleen. Using robotic instruments, traction, counter traction, and elevation, the surgeon seals and divides these vessels along the greater curvature of the stomach, preparing for the fundoplication.

The surgeon applies gentle pressure on the stomach while using the robotic vessel sealer to create a window and seal vessels near the spleen. The operation approaches the hiatus or gastroesophageal junction, where the top of the stomach connects to the esophagus. The area is being freed to ensure clear identification of the diaphragm muscles for re-approximation.

The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper to separate the esophagus from the left crux of the diaphragm, applying pressure in the opposite direction to achieve traction and counter traction. The surgical field shows the entry into the pars flaccida, with the identification of the right crux of the diaphragm.

The surgeon uses the vessel sealer to open the pars flaccida, revealing the inferior vena cava (IVC) beneath the liver. The esophagus is being carefully separated from the right crux of the diaphragm with counter traction, aiming to preserve the diaphragm muscles and identify the vagus nerve. The surgeon connects dissection points from the left to the right side of the patient.

The surgeon identifies the crux of the diaphragm, entering the thoracic cavity and carefully dissecting small perforators and vessels to free the area. The esophagus is separated from surrounding tissue, ensuring its mobility and confirming the connection between the stomach and esophagus is within the abdominal cavity. Adhesions are freed to prevent the hernia from being pulled back up.

The surgeon identifies the left and right crura of the diaphragm and frees the connections between the esophagus and diaphragm. An Ethabon suture is placed laparoscopically to prepare for closing the diaphragmatic hernia. The robotic assistance enhances visualization and facilitates the repair process.

The surgeon uses a simple slip knot to approximate the diaphragmatic muscles without strangulating them, carefully placing sutures near the esophagus to avoid dysphagia. After securing the sutures with several throws, the vessel sealer is used to cut the suture, demonstrating a cost-saving technique.

The surgeon cinches a slip knot and alternates throws to secure the repair, ensuring the sizing tube in the esophagus is not constricted to avoid discomfort or dysphagia. The hiatal hernia is closed to contribute to the anti-reflux mechanism. The surgeon prepares a window for the Nissen fundoplication, removing adhesions to prevent ischemia and ensure a clear area for the wrap, using a grasper to maintain the window.

The surgeon visually assesses the color of the liver, spleen, and surrounding areas, then grasps the fundus of the stomach with a grasper. The stomach is pulled through to create a 360-degree wrap, ensuring it is loose and floppy to prevent ischemia or stenosis. The surgeon identifies the stomach and prepares to suture it to the esophagus, using a shoe shine maneuver to maintain the wrap's mobility. The first suture is placed high, just below the gastroesophageal junction, securing a healthy portion of the fundus on the patient's left side. A stabilizing suture is placed through the esophagus, followed by a third suture on the right side of the stomach, completing the Nissen fundoplication wrap.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer as scissors to cinch down and secure the sutures, recreating the sphincter mechanism to prevent food and acid from entering the esophagus. The top of the fundoplication is initiated with alternating throws of Ethabon suture, ensuring the sutures are snug but not too tight.

The surgeon uses a robotic system to perform suturing for the Nissen fundoplication, alternating throws to recreate the sphincter mechanism at the top of the stomach. The surgeon employs a slip knot technique, using a large needle driver to snug down the sutures and a vessel sealer to cut and remove extra suture material. The assistant removes suture from the port while the surgeon continues suturing, incorporating a healthy portion of the esophagus to prevent slippage of the hiatal hernia.

The surgeon uses a thirty-degree scope pointed downward to visualize the newly created Nissen fundoplication, ensuring the wrap is loose. A gastropaxi suture is placed at the top of the Nissen through the fundus and the right crus of the diaphragm using a slip knot technique to prevent slippage. The vessel sealer is used to manage the suture, and the assistant removes it. The surgeon injects ICG dye intravenously, utilizing the robot's firefly technique to check for ischemia in the wrap and visualize the stomach and esophagus.

The surgeon performs an intraoperative endoscopy to evaluate the esophagus and the newly created fundoplication wrap. The endoscope is carefully inserted to assess the function of the hiatus and ensure the wrap is not too tight. Insufflation is used to open the top of the gastroesophageal junction, and the surgeon visualizes the stomach and repair site, checking for healthy tissue color.

The surgeon uses an eight-millimeter port to flip the view and inspect the fundoplication wrap from below. The assistant closes the skin incisions, and the surgeon ensures that all gas is removed from the patient.
